text: The surgeon adopts a transperitoneal approach, mobilizing the ascending colon to expose Girota's fascia.
The surgeon begins the dissection at the inferior aspect of the surgical field and progresses cranially, carefully separating tissue layers.
The surgeon employs a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to separate tissue layers, progressing through the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum and retracts it medially to expose the inferior vena cava during the robotic-assisted laparoscopic partial nephrectomy.
The surgeon dissects the lateral edge of the inferior vena cava, progressively exposing more of its length within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection adjacent to the inferior vena cava, revealing the posterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon retracts the ureter and gonadal vessels anteriorly, creating space for further dissection in the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the psoas muscle and inserts an additional retracting arm to facilitate further dissection in a cephalad direction.
The surgeon continues dissection towards the renal hilum, gradually exposing the renal vein as it comes into view.
The surgeon employs a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to further separate tissue layers, continuing the exposure of the renal hilum and surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the renal vein and continues posterior dissection to reveal a single renal artery, visible against the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon exposes the renal artery, visible as a white vertical structure, during the dissection towards the renal hilum.
The surgeon exposes a sufficient length of the renal artery, preparing it for hilar clamping during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon excises the previously identified renal lesion, carefully removing the tissue from the surrounding kidney structure.
After completing the hyalodissection, the surgeon exposes the renal lesion, revealing its location and boundaries within the renal tissue.
The surgeon incises Girota's fascia, revealing underlying structures and facilitating further access to the renal area.
The surgeon identifies and clears away the perinephric fat surrounding the kidney, revealing the underlying renal structures.
The upper pole of the kidney is exposed, revealing the tumor surrounded by retained perinephric fat, which is being circumvented during the procedure.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to visualize the renal lesion, determining its exact dimensions and marking the line of excision on the kidney surface.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to visualize the renal lesion, aiding in precise identification and assessment of the lesion's dimensions.
The surgeon performs hilar clamping, initially clamping the renal artery followed by the renal vein, as part of the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon excises the renal lesion from the exposed upper pole of the kidney, carefully removing the tumor along with the surrounding perinephric fat.
The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to identify the plane of dissection around the renal lesion, ensuring precise separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon uses a suction device to gently deflect the renal cortex in an inferior direction, revealing underlying structures for further dissection.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps from the da Vinci XI system to elevate the renal lesion, providing clear exposure for further excision.
Following the removal of the renal lesion, the surgeon performs rhinoraphy, suturing the renal tissue to close the excision site.
The surgeon begins placing a hemostatic suture using a 3-0 V-Loc suture along the base of the excised renal lesion to control bleeding.
The surgeon inspects the excision defect for adequate hemostasis, closing any inadvertent entry into the collecting system if observed.
The surgeon withdraws the suture through the renal cortex, controlling it with a Hemilox suture after completing the basal layer.
The surgeon applies tension to the suture, enhancing its hemostatic effect to control bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs closure of the excision defect using a V-lock suture, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.
The surgeon removes the hilar clamping, first releasing the renal clamp and then the renal artery clamp, indicating the completion of vascular control during the nephrectomy.
The surgeon applies compression sutures to the renal tissue, visibly enhancing hemostasis and securing the closure of the surgical defect.
The surgeon applies a second hemlock clip to secure the previously placed hemlock clip and V-lock suture, ensuring stability and preventing slippage.
The surgeon retrieves the nephrectomy specimen by placing it into a retrieval bag for removal from the surgical field.
The surgeon applies Flowseal onto the edge of the surgical defect, likely to promote hemostasis and seal the area.
The surgeon closes the gyrotous fascia over the top of the surgical site, completing the closure of the area.
